data.title: Polaris Renewable Energy: Share Price Has Detached From The Outlook
data.description:
Polaris Renewable Energy (PIF:CA) (RAMPF) is a renewable electricity producer with assets in South America and the Caribbean.

In October 2023, I wrote an article on Polaris highlighting its growth, dividend, and buybacks. This year Polaris is likely reaching a new production record; the dividend has remained unchanged as expected, but buybacks have been minimal. Polaris’ market cap and enterprise value are now lower despite higher energy production, revenue, and EBITDA.

The shares have only been a good vehicle for swing trading since the stock has stubbornly fluctuated between C$11-C$14. Those who have been patient enough to wait have enjoyed a high dividend yield. Now, the shares are again trading at the lower end of the range. The earnings are distorted by several accounting items, possibly scaring off some investors.

Polaris has its primary listing in Canada, and shares are trading in Canadian dollars (C$). It derives most of its revenue in U.S. dollars ($) and reports its figures in the same currency.

Company recap

Polaris owns and operates geothermal, hydro, solar, and wind power assets in five South American countries and Puerto Rico. In 2024, Polaris posted revenue of $76 million and adjusted EBITDA of $55 million. Both metrics came in lower than the year before. The key risk related to Polaris has been and still is the heavy geographical concentration risk of Nicaragua. The diversification has likely progressed slower than many investors hoped for.

Diversification is likely to take significant steps

Although the reliance is still high, Nicaragua’s share of power generation has decreased down to 55%. In 2024 the share was 61%. While the years 2023-2024 were fairly quiet in terms of business development, in March Polaris took a significant step in diversification by acquiring an equity interest in a 26MW wind park in Punta Lima, Puerto Rico.

Punta Lima has contributed to the results since the end of Q1 2025. In the first nine months, Punta Lima’s share was 5% of the total power production despite unfavorable wind conditions in Q3. The acquisition might be risky; however, the price per megawatt sold is 50-100% higher than what Polaris realizes with its other assets and includes an attractive reinvestment opportunity.

Thanks to the Punta Lima acquisition and favorable hydrological conditions in Peru and Ecuador, Polaris is likely to reach a record consolidated power production in 2025. In the first nine months, revenue increased 7% to $61 million. Adjusted EBITDA rose 4%, from $41.5 million to $43.2 million. Net cash flow from operating increased 12%, from $26 million to $29.2 million.

Despite fairly good headline figures, the shares declined sharply after the Q3 results. This is likely explained by what happened at the lower levels of the income statement. Due to one-time expenses related to early refinancing, higher interest expenses ($1 million), and slightly higher D&A, Polaris’ earnings were negative. On top of that, Polaris recorded a deferred tax increase from $0.4 million to $6 million due to an end of tax holidays in Nicaragua. Due to the aforementioned items, Polaris posted a negative earnings of $8.5 million compared to a profit of $6 million in the first nine months.

Polaris has retained a relatively strong balance sheet. Its net debt-to-EBITDA ratio stands at 2.5x. Its net debt has fluctuated around $120 million for the past year. Currently it holds a total cash balance of $99 million, enabling investments into existing and new assets. Cash position is historically high after issuing a large bond approximately one year ago.

Some shareholders have hoped that Polaris would execute meaningful share buybacks. The company has not purchased its own shares in any meaningful way, although it still has an authorization to purchase 10% of its 21 million outstanding shares. In Q3, the company purchased back a mere 27,000 shares. It appears that the company prefers investing and expanding its business, which is good from the perspective of diversification.

We move forward with hopefully 1 or 2 other projects next year, and we will, for sure, I would say, be giving more market updates and press releases as we move forward with these projects. So it will be more sort of -- call it, newsy on the development and construction activities next year. And I think it's important because those will be very material for the company. -CEO Marc Murnaghan, Q3 earnings call.

Polaris has a list of organic investment opportunities at attractive yields. One of the most actual and interesting ones is the so-called ASAP program in Puerto Rico, which means adding a battery energy storage system (BESS) to the existing site. Polaris is looking at an EBITDA uplift of $13-14 million by an investment of $50 million starting from the end of 2026. Polaris is contractually obligated to do the investment. The system would bring a new capacity-based revenue stream to Polaris’ portfolio. For new investments, Polaris is primarily scouting assets in its existing jurisdictions. A new asset is likely to be a solar power plant.

Discount to history and peers is rather large

According to Koyfin, Polaris is trading at an EV/EBITDA multiple of 5.2x, P/B of 0.7x, and P/FCF of 4.7x. The 5-year historical averages stand at 7x, 0.9x, and 19.2x. Today, Polaris is more diversified, and it is likely to post record revenue and EBITDA in 2025. The valuation is likely suppressed by the accounting items that have caused negative earnings but which have only a limited impact on cash flow.

The company has an attractive organic growth plan, and the management appears optimistic about new small- to mid-sized acquisitions. This is an encouraging sign for continuing growth. With its refinanced balance sheet, Polaris can add assets even without taking on more debt. Its ambitious goal of doubling the EBITDA in five years will require external funding. If there are signs of accelerating EBITDA growth, the market is likely to reprice the stock since relevant peers typically trade at twice higher multiples with higher leverage.

Polaris’ balance sheet is rather clean, but its book value has inched lower due to the accounting items. If the P/B multiple were to expand to its historical average of 0.9x, the share price would be $14.1, the upper end of its current trading range and representing a 20% upside. Typically, renewable energy companies trade at P/B multiples ranging from 1.1-2x.

There are five analysts following the company with an average target price of C$24, but this comes with a wide variation ranging from C$20 up to C$32.

A large dividend pays to wait

Polaris has maintained its dividend at $0.6 per share, which is divided into quarterly payments. At the current share price of C$11.7, the yield reaches up to 7.1%. The dividend payment consumes cash of $12.6 million annually, so it’s well covered by operating cash flow of around $39 million, leaving ample room for maintenance, finance, and growth expenditures.

Conclusion

Polaris shares might have reached a point where future outlook is overshadowed by short-term accountants. Considering business development, balance sheet valuation, and dividend, the current share price provides an attractive entry point. The main risk factor is likely to shrink in the coming years, and figures next year will likely look better as Punta Lima will have full effect and accounting items are in the rearview mirror.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: FNDF Over EFA: Escaping The Market-Cap Trap With Fundamental Indexing
data.description:
Amid a global divergence in central bank policies and the volatility linked to trade policies of the Trump administration, I believe that having a well thought out approach to global markets is crucial.

In this comparative analysis I will look at the Schwab Fundamental International Equity ETF (FNDF), given a buy rating for its “smart beta” approach rooted in fundamentals, and the iShares MSCI EAFE ETF (EFA), given a hold rating for its simple market-cap weighting strategy that I see as inferior to FNDF’s. EFA is not a bad fund and is effectively capturing the market’s beta in developed geographies, but the market cap strategy results in a strong exposure to expensive sectors and is excluding value powerhouses such as the industrial power of South Korea. The approach of FNDF, mixing sales, cash flow, and dividends metrics appears to be an outperformer, and as global markets are marked with supply chain reordering and potential valuation shifts, this positioning appears to be the best one.

The Strategic Advantage of Fundamental Weighting

What I like about FNDF is how its investment strategy is navigating the relation between a stock price and how it should be weighed in the portfolio. EFA is following a market-cap weighting strategy, which is a well admitted methodology in key benchmarks all over the world, but I don’t think that it is the best methodology to follow at all times. The reason behind that is the fact that the increasing share of passively managed funds in the markets is forcing investors to buy what everyone else is buying, inflating valuation multiples and making you buy expensive assets. FNDF is doing exactly the opposite: if the share price is growing faster than its fundamental metrics are (sales, cash flow, and dividends), the company’s weight in the portfolio is going to be reduced, and vice versa. Basically, it makes FNDF relevant when multiples are high. In the current ex US market environment, you will see in the chart below that multiples are above historical average levels (still not as high as in the US, but above average).

Given that the current macroeconomic environment is conflicted with localized recessions and tariff-induced volatility, I do believe that a disciplined approach to avoid paying growth premiums is relevant, and the focus away from market sentiment to economic footprints of FNDF appears to be especially relevant. Market-cap indexes like EFA are more relevant in my opinion when valuation multiples are low, because you will experience capital gains from growing multiples, but when valuation multiples are high you are directly exposed to the compression risk, while FNDF can be seen as a “valuation compression edge”.

In addition, EFA and FNDF don’t exactly use the same classification for developed countries, and this leads EFA and its MSCI EAFE index to exclude South Korea. I think that this is a big mistake as the country is home to tech giants like Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) and is a semiconductor powerhouse, at the center of the global AI supply chain: definitely a trend that you don’t want to miss out on. FNDF has a broader reach with a definition of developed markets that is, like its weighting methodology, more rooted in fundamentals.

The "Korea Gap" and Sector Divergence

Both FNDF and EFA are well diversified ETFs but FNDF’s portfolio has a broader reach thanks to its 907 portfolio companies, roughly 200 more than EFA’s 695. The breadth of FNDF is stronger, but the concentration level is actually similar: the top 10 of FNDF accounts for 13.38% of the portfolio against 12.96% for EFA.

One of the key differences in terms of geographic positioning lies in South Korea, as I mentioned before. The country accounts for 7.55% of FNDF’s portfolio, allowing the fund to benefit from an export-focused economy, central to the global chip demand. This is however not the only difference between the two ETFs.

As seen from the table above, FNDF is also heavy in Canada, and just like South Korea this is an economy with a strong export activity, and resource heavy. This makes FNDF exposed to industrial cycles and commodity prices themes, while the more European and Japanese focus of EFA positions it to have a more defensive positioning closer to traditional Western countries.

Looking at its European exposure, FNDF has a lower exposure than EFA to countries such as Switzerland, France, Germany, and the Netherlands that are reducing its footprint in pharmaceuticals, consumer staples, and industrial giants. The consequence for the fund’s profile is that it is relying less on lower‑beta “bond proxies” that are more representative of European economies, and makes the fund more about equity valuations and cyclicality in the local economy rather than a play on the dominance of established brands. This is clear when seeing that FNDF is giving a higher weight to the mid‑size European market with higher concentrations than EFA in countries such as Ireland, Austria, and Norway, away from the core European focus of EFA, consistent with its market-cap focus.

As hinted by the geographical split, EFA has a strong presence in the Financials segment, making it exposed to a potential compression of interest rates, even if it is for now not an immediate risk, as you can see from the German yield curve with long term interest rates rising despite the decreasing short term ones. FNDF has a different focus, with a lower exposure to interest rates but a stronger exposure to sectors with tangible assets, tied to the “real economy”, like with energy and durables.

EFA’s focus is more directly aimed at discretionary spending cycles, while the reach of FNDF towards sectors like process industries, non-energy minerals, and distribution services is tied with physical trade flows. Basically, from this comparison you can say that FNDF is an ETF that will especially benefit from a value rotation into hard assets, while EFA is a pure play on stable giants.

The Value Arbitrage

As a direct consequence from the two different weighting methodologies where EFA is buying overbought assets and FNDF is relying on fundamentals, the assets of FNDF are less expensive. The fund’s P/E ratio is 12.15 against 15.21 for EFA. This is a close to 20% valuation gap between the two funds, and as we know that the assets of FNDF are already filtered for quality and that the lower P/E is not a value trap, this provides us with a margin of safety. This is even more striking when looking at the P/B ratios: 1.27 for FNDF against 1.97 for EFA, a close to 55% gap between the two funds.

FNDF can be considered as a value play on global equities, and history teaches us that such a strategy is working better in times of high inflation volatility and economic recovery, which is not too far from the global economy currently. In addition, the value advantage of FNDF is seen in the fund’s higher dividend yield of 3.48%, slightly above EFA’s 3.40%. This annual stream difference is further deepened by the difference in expense ratios: 0.25% for FNDF against 0.32% for EFA. Given the large scale of EFA and its simple strategy, I think that the expense ratio is too high to be competitive, especially surprising because EFA is significantly larger and should leverage its scale to reduce costs.

EFA’s AUM is $69.9 Bn against “only” $19.4 Bn for FNDF. Indeed, this results in a better liquidity for EFA with a median bid/ask spread of 0.01% against 0.02% for FNDF, but this difference is only marginal and the higher AUM should be leveraged for better operational metrics by BlackRock. Here, it is not the case, and this is one of the reasons why EFA only earns a hold rating despite positive returns.

Performance Analysis

Let’s take a look at total returns:

As you can see, FNDF’s unique methodology has been a consistent outperformer against the market-cap benchmark. This methodology allows for a better capture of market dynamics, and I believe that this is going to be strengthened by a rotation in global markets towards “hard assets” (i.e. Energy and Industrials).

Naturally, total returns alone are not a good indicator, and we must oppose risk metrics to returns. First, the volatility, measured by standard deviations: 11.64% (3 years), 14.18% (5 years), and 15.62% (10 years) for FNDF and 12.61% (3 years), 15.11% (5 years), and 15.16% (10 years). There is a dual dynamic here: FNDF has been less volatile over the short to medium term and EFA has been less volatile on the long term. I think this is relevant with the “value” tilt of FNDF and the fact that the funds are designed for times of high inflation volatility and economic recovery, factors that were not as central as today before the pandemic. We can complete this with maximum drawdown to get a sense of the capital preservation of the two funds: over the last five years it was -22.76% for FNDF against -27.64%.

Now that we have this in mind, we can look at risk-adjusted returns with Sharpe ratios: 1.11 (3 years), 0.73 (5 years), and 0.50 (10 years) for FNDF against 0.81 (3 years), 0.45 (5 years), and 0.42 (10 years) for EFA. I think this is relevant to note that even on the 10Y time frame with a lower volatility for EFA, the risk-adjusted returns are still lower than those of FNDF.

Risks and Strategic Nuances

While I like the fact that FNDF’s is rooted in fundamentals away from the crowded trades of EFA, this also creates a different risk profile, very exposed to a recession risk. If the global environment turns to be recessionary, as FNDF is exposed to cyclical sectors, a decrease of the consumer demand will result in underperformance against the defensive sectors found in EFA’s portfolio, better positioned for capital preservation in a worst case scenario.

Also, the geographic exposure of FNDF, while overall better diversified, the South Korean exposure that I like might not be suitable for every investor as it gives you exposure to geopolitical tensions in the Korean peninsula, and if it is a source of worry, EFA is completely shielded from this risk, but it also means that you will miss on the AI supply chain advantage of the country, and I believe that the fundamental & value tilt of FNDF is enough to offset this risk.

In opposition, I do think that EFA is more exposed to trade policies risk induced by the Trump administration, which might be counter-intuitive with the fact that I highlighted above that FNDF is more positioned on export-focused economies. The reason behind that is that the market-cap weighting methodology of EFA makes the fund more about multinational corporations, and such companies are in need of a frictionless cross-border trade global economy to operate efficiently. In the current economic environment with “de-globalization”, such global giants are facing threats on their margins.

Finally, I will mention the currency risks as none of the two funds are hedged for their foreign currency exposure. Right now, as trade policies are purposefully weakening the dollar, a strong argument in favor of global equities is the fact that results made in other currencies are stronger when converted in dollars. Of course, this is the ongoing trend and there is a risk of reversal: a potential strengthening of the dollar would hurt the fund’s returns.

Conclusion

The Schwab Fundamental International Equity ETF earns a buy rating thanks to its approach rooted in fundamentals that shows stronger metrics for value, and risk-adjusted returns, with a strategic positioning that is perfect to navigate an environment with potential supply chain shifts and valuation compression. The different market-cap methodology of the iShares MSCI EAFE ETF only earns a hold rating as this methodology skews the fund towards already expensive companies, mainly financials and defensive sectors, which is solid over the very long term but probably not the best positioning in the current market environment.

data.title: CommScope: Awaiting Closing And Special Dividend
data.description:
In August, I concluded that Amphenol (APH) was coming to the rescue for shareholders of CommScope (COMM). This followed CommScope announcing a $10.5 billion sale of its Connectivity & Cable Solutions business to Amphenol, a transaction designed to eliminate all net debt and preferred equity while leaving the business with remaining cash.

This conclusion and the remaining businesses created an interesting situation, with the sum-of-the-parts analysis looking quite favorable in relation to shares trading around the $15 mark, even as shares saw a massive jump on the back of the deal announcement.

Troubled Infrastructure

CommScope was founded in 1976 and has seen quite a tumultuous corporate history. Private equity firm Carlisle bought the business back in 2011, only to bring it public in 2013.

The company went public at around the $15 mark, with shares peaking at around the $40 mark in 2017/2018. An overleveraged business, following multiple M&A deals, found itself in real trouble, with its stock trading down to just a dollar in 2024. This came amidst struggles in end markets, including widely covered inventory destocking trends after supply chains opened up again in a post-pandemic environment.

This made that peak sales of around $8 billion had fallen towards the $5 billion mark in 2023, on which about a billion in EBITDA was reported. While those margins are not too shabby, the direction of sales growth and leverage was both very negative, as the company torched along an $8.7 billion net debt load, for a near 9 times leverage ratio.

With first quarter sales for 2024 falling by 30%, the outlook was very dismal, even as the company announced the sale of its Outdoor Wireless Networks and the Distributed Antenna Systems to Amphenol. This $2.1 billion deal was announced in the summer of 2024. While absolute leverage would come down, the progress in relative leverage was much less pronounced, leaving the fate of equity holders of CommScope out to dry.

Coming To Life

At the start of this year, CommScope reported a 7% fall in 2024 sales to $4.21 billion, with EBITDA reported at $756 million, all while the company still torched along a multi-billion debt load. However, things started to turn, with comparable first-quarter sales up 23% to $1.11 billion, as second quarter sales rose as much as 32% to $1.31 billion. The quarterly adjusted EBITDA number improved to $337 million, and with net debt down to $6.7 billion following the first deal with Amphenol, real progress was made on this front.

All this changed overnight as CommScope announced a $10.5 billion deal (with net proceeds seen at $10 billion) with Amphenol, involving the sale of its connectivity and cable solutions. With net debt and preferred equity combined standing close to $8 billion, this would result in about a $2 billion pro forma net cash position, equal to about $9 per share based on 227 million shares outstanding.

Outside this net cash position, CommScope would be left with its Ruckus and ANS activities. Ruckus actually saw second-quarter sales up 46% to $190 million, with operating profits reported at $21 million. ANS revenues rose as much as 65% to $322 million, with operating profits reported at $50 million.

Annualized, this makes for a $2 billion remainco, with operating profits seen around $300 million; as such, a business could be worth quite a bit as well. Even if I value these activities at just 1 times sales and 7 times operating income, that would be equal to $9 per share, for an $18 per share sum-of-the-parts analysis, and likely even more. The news sent shares up 80% to $14 per share back in August, in between a convincing sale and a solid second-quarter earnings report announced.

Delivering

Having held an upbeat view on the shares in August, I was erring on the positive side but did not want to chase the shares. After all, I have been unfamiliar with the business while there were still quite some question marks outstanding.

Since the summer, shares have gradually moved higher, having traded in a $15-$20 range and now trading at $18 per share. Other than some regular corporate news releases, it has been rather quiet on the corporate front.

By mid-October, investors approved the deal with Amphenol, paving the road for that transaction to close in the coming period. By the end of the month, the company reported third-quarter sales with RemainCo sales reported at $516 million, compared to a $512 million number in the second quarter.

Ruckus reported third quarter sales at $178 million, up just 15% on the year before, with segment profits reported at just $19 million. ANS revenues of $337 million were up 77%, actually accelerating, with segment profits reported at $25 million. This indicates that RemainCo posts revenues at a run rate of $2 billion but now sees operating income at less than $200 million, compared to a $300 million estimate in August.

Net debt actually has come down to $6.55 billion, ahead of preferred equity outstanding and ten billion in net proceeds from the deal with Amphenol. That deal is now seen closing in the first quarter of 2026, with a special dividend seen payable 60-90 days upon closing, although the level of the dividend has not been communicated yet.

The company guided for remaining adjusted EBITDA between $350 and $375 million in all of 2025. This compares to a $280 million number in the first nine months and a $90 million number in the third quarter. This suggests that the fourth-quarter romance is coming down a bit on a sequential basis, with EBITDA seen between $70 and $75 million.

In between shares having done reasonably well since August, while the performance of RemainCo seems pressured a bit, I am very cautious here as the risk-reward has deteriorated a bit. All this makes me await more clarity, which I expect upon the release of the fourth quarter results and/or deal closing.

data.title: COPX: Copper Outlook And Long-Term AI, Energy Transition Tailwinds Support Rally
data.description:
As the backbone of the global energy transition as well as the ongoing AI revolution, copper has transformed from being a pure industrial metal into a strategic commodity. The metal is facing unprecedented demand on one hand while being vulnerable to low supply caused by production stops in key mines. Global X Copper Miners ETF (COPX) is one way we can get exposure to this strategic commodity, and it has been on a roll this year, as we can see:

We will give our overview of the fund, compare it to peer funds, and discuss the ongoing short- and long-term catalysts for the fund, the metal, as well as valuation and potential risks. Let’s go.

The Fund

Global X Copper Miners ETF's main goal is to provide investors exposure to a variety of copper miners and therefore exposure to the trends and weaknesses of the copper market, which has proven to be a key element in the new digital revolution surrounding AI and data center usage, but more on that later; let’s focus on the fund for now. The ETF seeks to replicate the performance, before fees and expenses, of the Solactive Global Copper Miners Total Return Index.

The weighting is based on the free float market cap of the constituents, with minimum criteria being $200 million if they are not constituents yet and $100 million if they are already in the fund, high daily turnover criteria of at least $500 thousand over the last three months, and a minimum of 75 thousand shares traded daily. The minimum number of companies in the fund is 20, and the maximum is 40, with a cap on individual company exposure of 4.75%.

We’ll focus on some key fund characteristics now, using the Seeking Alpha grading system. The fund has a Seeking Alpha C- Expense Grade, weighted down by the generally higher expense ratio of 0.65% compared to traditional broad funds, but also being highly rated in terms of Bid/Ask % Spread of a low 0.05%. Regarding liquidity, the fund has a high Seeking Alpha A Grade, with excellent daily share and dollar volume, as well as $4.21 billion AuM. The Dividend Grade is B-, mainly for its dividend growth rate for the past 5-year period.

The sector allocation is naturally tilted towards Basic Material and some residual of 2.77% for Industrials.

The fund offers broad international exposure, with Canada being the most represented country, with around 38.7% of the fund’s assets being Canadian companies, followed by China with 9.8%, United States with 9.5%, Australia and Japan are the fourth and fifth countries with the most exposure, with 8.3% and 6.3%, respectively.

Compared to peers in the industry, COPX is generally best positioned. The fund offers superior Momentum, Liquidity and Expenses characteristics compared to the other funds, with only Sprott Junior Copper Miners ETF (COPJ) having a better Dividends and Risk grade, but just marginally.

When it comes to price performance, there is really no debate here: COPX has been the clear winner. However you want to look at the picture, long-term and short-term, COPX has delivered the best performance so far.

AI and Energy Transition as Long-Term Tailwinds, Supply Disruptions, China Story

Moving on to the main part of the analysis, the key catalysts for the period ahead. One significant structural driver of increasing copper demand is the expansion and integration of AI data centers. Power demand from data centers is expected to increase by 165% by the end of the decade, according to Goldman Sachs. And data centers need copper throughout their capacity chain, from cooling to connectivity needs. AI hyperscaler data centers use vastly more copper than traditional facilities, 10x to 3x as much copper to be exact. On that note, copper demand, according to Bloomberg NEF, just from new data centers will require an average of 400 thousand metric tons of copper annually over the next decade, while cumulatively that figure is around 4.3 million tons. On the other side, copper supply is expected to reach 29 million tons by 2035, meaning that the gap is projected to widen, creating conditions for continued price acceleration. Continuing with supply, that projection isn't really that concrete, taking into account the recent supply crunches related to copper we have seen, like the mudslide at Freeport's (FCX) Grasberg mine (which happens to be the world's second-largest copper mine). Getting these copper mines up and running hasn't really been a smooth ride, as some mines aren't expected to go back to pre-disruption production till 2027. Regarding long-term demand for copper, we also have to emphasize the ongoing energy transition, and as this research by BHP shows, copper will and does play a critical role in technologies such as wind, solar, hydro, and electric vehicles. Just as an example, the transport sector's share of total copper demand is expected to increase from 11% in 2021 to over 20% in 2043, while copper is projected to increase.

Structurally, China's story for copper isn't ending anytime soon, with China being the top consumer of copper, but more importantly, as BHP notes, it has only half of the accumulated stock per capita of the metal compared to developed countries. This translates to continuing demand for the metal as China continues its journey.

Valuation

Even though the fund has had a fantastic year, the P/E ratio has actually contracted, dropping from 29.66x in 2024 to 26.13x in 2025, which means that earnings expansion has outpaced price growth. Being a capital-intensive industry, it’s also important to compare the P/B ratio, which has also fallen compared to the past year, from 2.13x to 2.03x.

The top 10 constituents together form around 50% of the fund’s assets, with the top holding having around 5.8%. What is a likely takeaway is the relatively high gross margin for the companies, elevated valuation compared to their 5-year average, and positive expected revenue growth, which somewhat supports the valuation metrics given that this is a highly cyclical industry.

The fund is supported by strong technical drivers, blasting past the 20, 50, and 200-Day Moving Averages.

Risks

Regarding traditional demand, it has been severely impacted by tariff and trade uncertainty, with frontloading pushing the metal to new highs in anticipation of preventing impact from renewed tariff announcements from the U.S. administration. This has been supportive for copper miners, but we have to acknowledge that this could swing either way, as was the case in August with the short-lived tariff scare.

The metal's short-term performance is also under the impact of the biggest economy; it has the largest consumer, and Chinese PMI data hasn't been really perfect in the last couple of months, with Manufacturing PMI declining. Another short-term risk is the potential comeback of the US dollar, which has had a rough year, but a dollar comeback is negatively correlated to the performance of the metal.

Conclusion

Global X Copper Miners ETF has been a winning performer for 2025, supported by both structural and short-term drivers. The fund presents an adequate and, in my opinion, superior investment vehicle compared to peer funds, with best-in-class past price performance and better overall Seeking Alpha ratings. The fund is majorly dependent on the price movement of copper, which is expected to have long-term tailwinds arising from the AI revolution and the energy transition on the way, while short-term risks arise from the tariff uncertainty and trade conditions, resulting in frontloading, which has been a top catalyst for this year but could present oversupply issues in the period ahead. Given everything we have so far discussed, I give the fund a long-term BUY rating while being cautious short term of said risks.

data.title: Cobalt Miners News For The Month Of December 2025
data.description:
Welcome to the December 2025 cobalt miners news.

The past month saw cobalt prices higher.

Cobalt price news

As of December 18, the cobalt spot price was at US$23.94/lb, higher than US$22.03/lb last month. The LME cobalt price is US$51,865/tonne. LME Cobalt inventory is 123 tonnes, slightly down from 126 last month. More details on cobalt pricing (in particular the more relevant cobalt sulphate), can be found here at Benchmark Mineral Intelligence or Fast Markets MB.

Cobalt spot prices long-term price chart—USD 23.94/lb (source)

Cobalt demand vs. supply forecasts

You can view the latest Trend Investing cobalt demand vs. supply article here.

The new DRC export quota system could potentially flip the ex-DRC market balance from a cobalt surplus to a deficit in 2025 (RHS chart) (source)

Trend Investing vs. IEA 2021 demand forecast for EV metals (IEA)

Cobalt market news

On December 3, Reuters reported:

Congo's attempt to control cobalt market risks becoming supply shock... February's export ban was replaced with a quota system in October but the country's producers are still awaiting the green shipping light from Congo's minerals authority ARECOMS. The withdrawal of supply from the country that accounted for 70% of global mine production last year has been a booster for the bombed-out cobalt price...As the supply hiatus lengthens, the impact on the cobalt processing chain grows. Even if Congo's exports resumed tomorrow, the material would only arrive in China towards the end of the first quarter of next year. A drawdown of Chinese cobalt hydroxide stocks was always anticipated, given Congo's allocated export quotas for the next two years are around half the country's previous shipping levels. But the seven-week delay since the quota announcement will serve only to accentuate the inventory depletion.

On December 3, Fastmarkets reported:

US critical minerals security drive brings new era of stockpiling, project investment.

  • The US government has ramped up its efforts to stockpile critical materials for its national stockpile, but has faced challenges in doing so.
  • Many requests by the DLA have been extended or cancelled, demonstrating the challenges of obtaining critical materials, many of which with defense applications.
  • Alongside its efforts to stockpile critical and defense metals, the US government is utilising other tools of industrial support by supporting international projects to boost domestic supply.

On December 4, the US Department of State announced:

data.title: VFVA: This Regime Doesn't Favor Value Anymore
data.description:
The Vanguard U.S. Value Factor ETF Shares (VFVA), launched on 02/13/2018 and managed by The Vanguard Group, Inc., provides exposure to U.S. equities with value characteristics across most size segments. It manages $750 million and charges an expense ratio of 0.13%.

When I first covered VFVA more than a year ago, I only focused on the fact that this ETF has a short track record and that the lack of a size segment focus should not favor it in the long run. In this article, I want to talk more about what makes such a fund likely to underperform in the short run due to macro conditions that should favor growth stocks.

First, it's fair to say that some value ETFs, which I only recently rated as buys, had the benefit of focusing on large caps and overweighting some of the largest U.S. corporations. This is not the case here. The top 10 holdings don't contribute to the portfolio to a very meaningful degree, and none of them is one of the largest 10 companies by market capitalization. A couple of months ago, I would have considered this an advantage due to the level of diversification, but the risk profile of the market has changed, as we'll see shortly.

The sector weights also point to a relative disadvantage now. More conservative and often considered undervalued sectors like Health Care and Financials account for a large part of the portfolio. These are likely to underperform Tech stocks, which only account for 11%.

Usually, I am able to safely assume that a value ETF can do better when uncertainty is rising and the market starts pricing some risks that it has been ignoring. This was the case back in April; it was no secret that the Trump administration was going to announce higher tariffs on imports and potentially start a trade war with other major economies. And yet, the market reacted as if it didn't take that risk seriously. Many value funds experienced more modest drawdowns than the broader equity market and growth portfolios. VFVA did not, which points to some structural difference with other value strategies.

In any case, this underperformance should persist going forward, and this is where the macro lens comes in.

First of all, even though Real GDP growth improved further in the third quarter after the contraction realized in the first one, economists still expect Real GDP to grow at a slower pace than in the recent past and long-term average levels. This is consistent with the view that the recovery in the second and third quarters is due to the deceleration of imports that followed inventory front-loading prior to the higher tariffs. Therefore, the implication is that the trend that is forming cannot be trusted in this context. The average Real GDP growth in the first half of the year was about 1.4%, and this is more reflective of the expectations that the economy is slowing after you account for the import distortion.

A more reliable trend can be observed in the index of new orders for manufacturing goods based on the related ISM survey. With only a brief recovery above 50 a few months back and the index persistently below it, the slowing aggregate demand supports the forecasts of below-average growth levels in the near term.

The direct business impact here implies a relative inferiority of value stocks. The underlying businesses are more tied to cyclical demand drivers than secular ones (e.g., Financials, Consumer Cyclical, and Energy). Sentiment doesn't shift simply because of changes in the last quarter's Real GDP figures, but the bottom-line differential should be enough to affect sentiment and punish value stocks more than growth ones in such a regime, all else being equal.

The labor market's softening further supports this regime as very likely. Nonfarm payroll growth fell from about 1.2% at the start of the year to 0.6% lately on a YoY basis. The unemployment rate has also been rising rapidly and now sits at 4.6%. Moreover, the number of unemployment insurance claims has stayed elevated and was recently reported at 224K, indicating more unemployment.

This also implies that the Federal Reserve is going to keep cutting at the new pace it has stuck to since September. The labor market is now the bigger risk, and monetary policy should prioritize it over inflation. Besides, CPI and Core CPI YoY growth seem to have stabilized below 3%. The idea was that with the new tariff rates, businesses were going to pass on the higher input cost to consumers as soon as the pre-tariff inventory ran out. The trend after April reflects this assumption, but the level is now closer to the Fed's target of 2% and helps to allow the central bank to be less concerned about inflation getting out of control again if it cuts further.

This is not a headwind for value stocks, of course, but this outlook favors growth stocks more, as they are more sensitive to interest rate changes.

Before the higher tariffs were enacted and the trade wars, the case for value strategies was stronger primarily because of sentiment and sticky inflation that maintained the high uncertainty. Now that the market is desensitized to some extent and the labor market is obviously the bigger risk for the Fed, value stocks have lost their edge. It's highly unlikely that these conditions will allow value portfolios to outperform the broader market and growth stocks in the near term. For this reason, I am maintaining my hold rating on VFVA.

Now, I am always open to challenging my views because being an ideologue in investing and trading can prove expensive. So if you have a different opinion, this platform is the right place to discuss both strategy and the various options with which we can position our portfolios. Go ahead and leave a comment below to let me know what you think.

Otherwise, please let me know if you found this article useful. It means a lot!

data.title: Gift BOXX Under The Tree Brings Holiday Cheers And No Tax Fears
data.description:
The Alpha Architect 1-3 Month Box ETF (BOXX) is best understood as a short-term interest rate instrument wrapped in a tax-efficient ETF wrapper. It seeks to “equal or exceed the price and yield performance” of the 1–3 month U.S. Treasury bill sector, before fees and expenses. Instead of owning T-bills, it gets there by packaging box spreads, a multi-leg options strategy, so the fund’s return shows up primarily as NAV accretion and price appreciation rather than a steady stream of taxable interest distributions.

BOXX invests in box spreads with a weighted average maturity of less than 90 days. The fund expects to trade some or all positions prior to expiration and roll into new spreads. The prospectus also states the fund uses European-style options, which matters because European-style options can be exercised only at expiration, eliminating early assignment risk. So we can say that BOXX is like a ladder of very short-term “synthetic bills,” refreshed continuously.

Understanding Box Spreads

A box spread uses put-call parity to engineer a guaranteed fixed payoff at maturity. Conceptually, you create two vertical spreads with the same strikes and expiry: a bull call spread and a bear put spread. Note also that these must be European options so that there is no risk of early assignment, and in nearly all cases we are using SPX index options because these are the most liquid European options. Since some legs of the box spread are always going to be ITM, this is a very important stipulation

If you choose strikes K_1 and K_2, where K_1 < K_2, and hold the combined position to expiry, the payoff is the same regardless of where the underlying ends up: it is simply K_2 – K_1. The New York Fed walks through this intuition in this article, noting that the combined payoff at maturity from the four-leg structure is constant and equal to the strike difference.

Once the payoff is fixed, the “interest rate” shows up in the price you pay today. You pay less than K_2 – K_1 today and receive K_2 – K_1 at maturity, and the difference is your implied lending return. Pay more today (possible in some market microstructure contexts), and you’ve effectively borrowed. The return of a box spread is basically the implied interest rate of engaging in this type of financial markets transaction.

Based on the no-arbitrage principle, that implied rate should sit very close to the risk-free rate for the same maturity because arbitrage should collapse persistent gaps. In practice, it won’t match T-bills perfectly. The “box spread rate” is an options market funding rate influenced by the supply and demand for financing through options, bid-ask spreads and market liquidity, and the mechanics of margins and clearing. In contrast to SPX options, T-bills are the most liquid instruments in the world and are viewed under various frameworks as high-quality liquid assets. That’s why box spread rates often trade rich to T-bills, as seen in this chart below. An illiquidity spread is applied to the box spread.

BOXX’s job is to harvest that box spread rate consistently at the front end of the curve and do it efficiently enough that, after fees and trading frictions, investors receive economic returns like that of a T-bill. One advantage of BOXX is that because the gains accrue to the NAV and are not distributed regularly, it actually offers better tax treatment compared to T-bills. Consider that holding a ladder of less than 3-month bills will incur a tax burden on the interest paid in the same year that it is paid. ETFs that hold floating rate notes or roll over short-term paper and pay out a steady dividend have the same problem. BOXX does not have this problem.

Tailwinds and Headwinds for BOXX

Because BOXX is economically a short-term rates instrument, macro factors matter more through the path of front-end rates, money-market plumbing, and the slope of the very short curve.

The Fed cut on December 10, 2025, and set the target range for the federal funds rate to 3.50%–3.75%. In the same neighborhood, FRED shows SOFR at 3.68% for December 22, 2025, and the three-month constant-maturity Treasury yield has been around the mid-3s recently. In that environment, BOXX should deliver “cash-like” returns in the mid-3s to high-3s before fees, with the exact realized number dependent on box spread rate execution, roll timing, and trading costs.

If the Fed continues easing, BOXX’s forward return compresses quickly. That’s a headwind in absolute terms (lower returns), and it is a headwind in relative terms because the expense ratio becomes a larger share of the gross yield. When cash yields are 0–1%, a ~0.19% net expense ratio becomes a meaningful tax on the strategy.

If the Fed pauses at restrictive-or-neutral levels, BOXX is in its sweet spot. “Higher for longer” keeps the gross carry high enough that net returns remain competitive after fees, while the low duration profile limits mark-to-market swings.

If inflation re-accelerates and the Fed hikes again, BOXX benefits. But the subtle risk is that hiking cycles can coincide with periodic money-market stress. In those episodes, the spread between box spread rates and T-bills can widen, and liquidity premia can show up in option financing. That doesn’t necessarily mean BOXX loses money—as long as the box spread is held several weeks to maturity and the OCC does not fail, it is guaranteed to pay off as expected—but it does mean tracking can become noisier at exactly the moments investors are most sensitive to “cash behaving oddly.”

During severe dislocations, bid-ask spreads widen and rolling costs can increase. The payoff of a held-to-expiry box is fixed, but the fund’s actual implementation includes rolling and active trading, so short-term frictions matter more than they would for someone who personally put on a box spread and held it to maturity. To be clear, I don’t see this as a real concern, but it is something that is good to be aware of.

Ultimately the counterparty for options is the OCC, which is recognized as a systemically important financial market utility (SIFMU). This is highly, highly unlikely to have serious issues.

Tax Mechanics

As I mentioned, BOXX’s differentiator is its tax packaging. Instead of distributing interest, it allows NAV to rise, so holders realize taxable gain mostly when they sell, potentially as capital gains rather than ordinary income. For investors with taxable cash allocations—especially those who can hold longer than a year—tax deferral plus long-term capital gains treatment can be meaningfully better than current taxation of money market distributions.

BOXX is structured as a regulated investment company. Under the RIC rules, the shareholder is usually the primary taxpayer, not the fund—provided the fund meets the qualification and distribution requirements. ETFs also have a structural advantage that can reduce realized gains via the in-kind creation and redemption mechanism. When an ETF meets redemptions by handing out securities in kind, it can avoid realizing gains inside the fund, avoiding having to distribute gains to shareholders in the current year.

Where BOXX becomes unusual is that its return comes from box spreads. The tax outcome depends on which options it uses in the in-kind creation and redemption process. Index options are treated as Section 1256 contracts, which have a 60/40 split between long- and short-term capital gains. This also forces annual recognition of gains and losses for those contracts, which increases the odds of taxable distributions.

So what does BOXX do?

The answer is it selects certain contracts as part of its strategy in the in-kind creation and redemption process so as to manage the realizations. What ends up happening is that the fund does not have taxable gains even though it has consistent economic gains because it realizes losses while using in-kind transactions to not have to realize gains.

Overall, this is a really impressive use of the tax code to engineer a fund that can consistently grow at the risk-free rate without incurring regular taxes.

The larger issue is the durability and longevity of this situation. The IRS could close this “loophole” and potentially cause the fund to have to pass off distributions. Should the tax regime change, the basic economics of BOXX could be slightly altered.

BOXX itself flags this kind of risk in its prospectus, and ETF.com describes the strategy as tax arbitrage that could face a regulatory response.

At this point, I would also like to note that because BOXX ultimately uses Section 1256 contracts (SPX options) to comprise the box spreads, any future distributions—if mandated by a change in the tax code—would pass the 60/40 treatment through to the investor. This would still make it superior to the likes of SGOV, USFR, and TFLO (which also pay short-term risk-free rates), which would be taxed as ordinary income, which is effectively the short-term capital gains tax rate.

Overall, the slightly higher rate on box spreads vs. treasuries has allowed it to do better than competitors over the last year. This is on a net of fees but before tax basis, so the after-tax basis is even better.

In summary, BOXX’s “NAV compounding” is enabled by its unique structure and the way options returns can be packaged via in-kind transactions, but the edge ultimately rests on tax law remaining favorable. However, even in an unfavorable tax regime, the use of index options will still create a slightly better tax treatment.

Risks

As mentioned, the investor is exposed to the OCC as the ultimate counterparty. This is perhaps worse than the U.S. government, which is what you’d get with T-bills. However, the OCC is systemically important, and that is a major margin of safety.

Second, BOXX could engage in active and frequent trading and expects high portfolio turnover. This has some execution risk. It is also slightly exposed to liquidity risk. If spreads widen from systemic stress, you can lose many basis points here and there, which could lead to underperformance against T-bills.

Third, the tax feature could change. If the IRS narrows the advantage, BOXX becomes a higher-fee, short-term, risk-free rate product and loses its edge versus SGOV.

Finally, there is the macro risk of lower rates at the front of the curve. With a higher fee, the investor nets a smaller portion of total returns.

Conclusion

BOXX is a clever wrapper around a well-understood options trade, and in today’s mid-3% short-term rates environment, it should behave like a useful cash surrogate. The fund’s defining edge is its highly favorable tax treatment. In that sense, it stands out as the best near-zero volatility, positive returns asset, beating out competitors like SGOV, USFR, and TFLO.

Obviously, BOXX will not outperform things with greater risk premia and growth potential. If rates fall, it should heavily underperform risk assets. But because I think BOXX is truly the best of its own category within the investment universe, I give it a Buy rating.

data.title: Preparing For An Anemic 2026 Market
data.description:
Introduction

In January of each year, I perform an annual assessment of my overall portfolio and rebalance. Anyone who has studied risk management understands that to manage risk, one should do occasional rebalancing. First, I have to answer up to 19 questions from The Smart Money Guide to Long-Term Investing: How to Build Real Wealth for Retirement and Other Future Goals (2003), by Nellie Huang and Peter Finch. I used their questions, adapted the results to align with more modern allocation theories, and developed an allocation tool that I use for myself and anyone who asks for my help.

Here we go.

The Questions

These questions must be answered before anyone can understand their needs and how to allocate their money. Excuse my brief rant. Advisors who are not asking these kinds of questions give you a short survey, and show a card with six sample portfolios need to be fired. The job of an advisor is to understand the answers to the following questions.

Initial Considerations

  • How old are you? This is a no-brainer. Once one reaches age 59 ½, the need for a fixed income increases with each passing year. For each year beyond 59 ½, add one percentage point to their fixed-income allocation.
  • When will you need the money? Another obvious question. If one’s retirement age is less than 30 years away, then they should take less risk with each year that passes. That means that as one approaches retirement, they need to add 1% to their fixed-income allocation. If retirement is more than 30 years away, make no changes.
  • Portfolio requirements will change your overall allocation and reduce your fixed-income and equity allocations.
    • Will you consider hedge funds? I suggest no more than 15% is invested in hedge funds.
    • Will you consider REITs? I suggest no more than 6% toward real estate.
    • Will you consider gold and other commodities? I suggest no more than 6% in gold.
    • Will you consider international investments? For years, global equities have lagged, but 2025 proved that one should consider investing beyond the United States.

How Much Fixed Income

How one answers these questions will change one’s fixed-income needs. I hope I can explain it well. Please note that anyone with a pension will need to make significant adjustments to these calculations, since a pension functions as a fixed income.

  • What is the size of your portfolio? If one’s portfolio is greater than $640,000, then no adjustment is necessary for their fixed-income requirements. If it is less than $128,000, they need to add 3% to their fixed-income allocation. For any amount in between those amounts, they need to add 1%.
  • How much are you contributing toward investing per year? If you contribute more than $24,000 per year, no fixed-income adjustment is necessary. If you contribute only up to $7,500 per year, then add 2% to your fixed income. For any other amount, add 1%.
  • What percent of your portfolio is in tax-deferred accounts? If the answer to this question is less than 25%, then add 1% to your fixed-income allocation. If it exceeds 75%, add 3% to your fixed income. For any other percentage, add 2%.

Spending Needs

It is essential to understand how much of one's money will be needed over the next 10 years. Here are the questions that need to be answered.

  • How many people, such as a child, spouse, or aging parent, are financially dependent on you? If you have dependents, add 1% to your fixed income for each dependent, up to a maximum of 3.
  • What percentage of your investments will you spend in the next three years? If this answer is zero, then reduce your fixed income by 1%. If it is less than 9%, add 1%. If it is between 9% and 18%, add 5%. If it is more than 18%, add 8%.
  • What percentage of your investments will you spend in the next ten years? If this answer is zero, then reduce your fixed income by 1%. If it is less than 30%, make no changes. If it is between 30% and 60%, add 4%. If it is more than 60%, then add 6%.
  • How much equity do you have in your home? If you are a renter, add 2% to your fixed-income allocation. Once you buy a house and take on debt, the equation changes a little. If you own less than 1/3 of the home’s equity, add 4%. If you own more than 1/3, then add 1%. If you own your home free and clear, then make no changes.

Current Income

This is pretty basic. If you are in a low-income tax bracket, add 3% to your fixed income. If you are in a middle-income tax bracket, add 2%. High-income earners do not have to make any changes.

Volatility Tolerance

This is important. If you do not know your risk tolerance, I suggest taking the Grable & Lytton Risk Tolerance Scale. It is an excellent assessment and has been peer-reviewed for its validity. After you take the quiz, you will find out whether you are:

  • Very Conservative—add 10% to fixed income
  • Conservative—add 6% to fixed income
  • Moderate—add 2% to fixed income
  • Aggressive—subtract 2.5% from fixed income
  • Very Aggressive-subtract 7% from fixed income

Economic Outlook

This will be a somewhat personal question, but it should be rooted in evidence. How one views the economy and markets over the next 12 months will affect their fixed income allocation. These are the adjustments one should make.

  • Turning Down—add 8% to fixed income
  • Anemic Growth—add 4% to fixed income
  • Strong Low Inflation—add 3% to fixed income
  • Strong High Inflation—add 2% to fixed income
  • Don’t Know—make no changes. In normal circumstances, this is my default choice, but I believe 2026 will be anemic.

A lot of math and algorithms take over from here when I run this tool for myself, and they are based on the work of Huang and Finch. The same questions above also determine how much I will allocate toward large caps, mid-caps, small caps, and foreign equities. As for the alternative investments—gold, real estate, and hedge funds—they are proportional to my age relative to my retirement timeframe, fixed-income allocation, and equity allocation.

I plan to retire at the end of the year, and I consider myself an aggressive investor. We also own our home free and clear. Reserving any additional information, this is the allocation that is generated for me. It includes 46% in cash, cash equivalents, and fixed income.

  • Cash—4%
  • Cash Equivalents—15%
  • Investment Grade Bonds—9%
  • High Yield Bonds—9%
  • International Bonds—9%
  • Large-Cap Stocks—11%
  • Mid-Cap Stocks—10%

  • Developed Markets—8%

  • Real Estate—2%
  • Gold or Precious Metals—2%
  • Hedge Funds—8%

I am satisfied with the outcome of this allocation when I use the relevant indices for January 1995 to October 2025. If you will allow me to brag, these numbers make this an excellent allocation.

This is due to its limited market exposure (0.51).
